content.text: Mind precedes all mental states. Mind is their chief; they are mind-made. If one speaks or acts with an impure mind, suffering follows him even as the wheel follows the hoof of the ox.
Mind precedes all mental states. Mind is their chief; they are mind-made. If one speaks or acts with a pure mind, happiness follows him like his shadow that never leaves him.

content.sanskrit: Akkocchi mam avadhi mam ajini mam ahasi me
content.text: 'He abused me, he struck me, he defeated me, he robbed me'—in those who harbor such thoughts, hatred is never appeased.

content.sanskrit: Akkocchi mam avadhi mam ajini mam ahasi me
content.text: 'He abused me, he struck me, he defeated me, he robbed me'—in those who do not harbor such thoughts, hatred is appeased.

content.sanskrit: Na hi verena verani sammantidha kudacanam
content.text: Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased. This is a law eternal.

content.sanskrit: Pare ca na vijananti mayametham yamamase
content.text: Others do not know that in this world we must all die. But those who realize this, their quarrels are calmed at once.
Whoever lives contemplating pleasant things, with senses uncontrolled, immoderate in eating, indolent, and inactive—him Mara certainly overthrows, as the wind overthrows a weak tree.

Whoever lives contemplating the impurities of the body, with senses well-controlled, moderate in eating, full of faith and strength—him Mara cannot overthrow, any more than the wind can overthrow a rocky mountain.
Whoever, not free from defilements, devoid of self-control and truth, should don the yellow robe—he is not worthy of the yellow robe.
But he who has discarded all defilements, who is well-established in moral virtues, possessed of self-control and truth—he is indeed worthy of the yellow robe.
Those who mistake the unessential for the essential and the essential for the unessential, never attain the essential, living in the pasture of wrong thoughts.

content.sanskrit: Saran ca sarato natva asaran ca asarato
content.text: Those who know the essential as the essential and the unessential as the unessential, attain the essential, living in the pasture of right thoughts.
As rain penetrates an ill-thatched house, so lust penetrates an undeveloped mind.

content.sanskrit: Yatha agaram succhannam vutthi na samativijjhati
content.text: As rain does not penetrate a well-thatched house, so lust does not penetrate a well-developed mind.

content.sanskrit: Idha socati pecca socati ubhayattha socati
content.text: The evil-doer grieves in this world, and he grieves in the next; he grieves in both worlds. He grieves and is afflicted, seeing the impurity of his own actions.

content.sanskrit: Idha modati pecca modati ubhayattha modati
content.text: The righteous man rejoices in this world, and he rejoices in the next; he rejoices in both worlds. He rejoices and is delighted, seeing the purity of his own actions.

content.sanskrit: Idha tappati pecca tappati ubhayattha tappati
content.text: The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both worlds. He suffers thinking 'I have done evil'; he suffers even more when gone to a state of misery.

content.sanskrit: Idha nandati pecca nandati ubhayattha nandati
content.text: The righteous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both worlds. He is happy thinking 'I have done good'; he is happier still when gone to a state of bliss.

content.sanskrit: Bahumpi ce samhitam bhasamano na takkaro hoti naro pamatto
content.text: Though he recites many sacred texts, if he does not act accordingly, that heedless man is like a cowherd counting others' cows; he has no share in the blessings of the monastic life.

content.sanskrit: Appampi ce samhitam bhasamano dhammassa hoti anudhammacari
content.text: Though he recites but a little of the sacred texts, if he lives according to the Dhamma, having discarded lust, hatred, and delusion, possessing right knowledge and a free mind, clinging to nothing in this world or the next—he shares in the blessings of the monastic life.
Heedfulness is the path to the Deathless; heedlessness is the path to death. The heedful do not die; the heedless are as if dead already.
Distinctly understanding this, the wise, rejoiced in heedfulness, delight in the pasture of the Noble Ones.
These wise ones, meditative, persevering, always struggling strenuously, attain to Nibbana, the supreme peace and freedom.
The glory of him who is energetic, mindful, pure in deed, considerate, self-controlled, living according to the law, and heedful, increases steadily.
By effort, earnestness, discipline, and self-control, let the wise man make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.
Fools, unwise people, give themselves up to heedlessness. The wise man, however, guards heedfulness as his greatest treasure.
Do not give yourselves up to heedlessness, nor to intimacy with sensual pleasures. The heedful and meditative man obtains abundant happiness.

When the wise man drives away heedlessness by heedfulness, ascending the tower of wisdom, he looks down upon the sorrowing crowd, himself free from sorrow, as one on a mountain looks down upon those on the plain.
Heedful among the heedless, awake among the sleeping, the wise man advances like a swift horse leaving a weak hack behind.
By heedfulness did Magha (Indra) attain the lordship of the gods. People praise heedfulness; heedlessness is always blamed.
A monk who delights in heedfulness and looks with fear on heedlessness moves about like a burning fire, consuming all fetters, great and small.
A monk who delights in heedfulness and looks with fear on heedlessness is not liable to fall away; he is close to Nibbana.
The fluttering, unsteady mind, difficult to guard and difficult to control, the wise man straightens, as a fletcher straightens an arrow.
Like a fish drawn from its watery home and thrown upon the land, this mind flutters all over, in order to escape the dominion of Mara.
It is good to control the mind, which is difficult to restrain, fickle, and turning to wherever it desires. A controlled mind brings happiness.
Let the wise man guard his mind, which is very difficult to perceive, extremely subtle, and wandering wherever it wishes. A guarded mind brings happiness.
Those who control their mind, which travels far, wanders alone, is bodiless, and hides in the chamber of the heart, will be free from the bonds of Mara.
If a man's mind is unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his serenity is troubled, his wisdom will never be perfect.
If a man's mind is free from lust, if his mind is free from hatred, if he has discarded both good and evil, there is no fear for such an awake one.

content.sanskrit: Kumbhupamam kayamimam viditva nagarupamam cittamidam thapetva
content.text: Knowing that this body is fragile like a jar, and making this mind firm like a fortress, let him fight Mara with the weapon of wisdom, guard what he has conquered, and be without attachment.
Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, bereft of consciousness, like a useless log.

content.sanskrit: Diso disam yan ta kayira veri va pana verinam
content.text: Whatever an enemy may do to an enemy, or a hater to a hater, an ill-directed mind does greater harm to oneself.

content.sanskrit: Na ta mata pita kayira anne va pi ca nataka
content.text: Neither mother, nor father, nor any other relative can do so much good as a well-directed mind.

content.sanskrit: Ko imam pathavim vijessati yamalokan ca imam sadevakam
content.text: Who shall conquer this earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods? Who shall find the well-taught path of virtue, even as a skilled garland-maker finds the right flower?

content.sanskrit: Sekho pathavim vijessati yamalokan ca imam sadevakam
content.text: The disciple will conquer the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find the well-taught path of virtue, even as a skilled garland-maker finds the right flower.
Knowing that this body is like froth, and understanding its nature to be like a mirage, let him break the flower-tipped arrows of Mara, and pass unseen by the King of Death.
The man who gathers flowers (of sensual pleasures), whose mind is distracted, death carries off, as a great flood sweeps away a sleeping village.
The man who gathers flowers, whose mind is distracted, who is insatiable in sensual pleasures—him the Destroyer brings under his sway.
As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower or its color or fragrance, so let the sage dwell in the village.

content.sanskrit: Na paresam vilomani na paresam katakatam
content.text: Let one not examine the faults of others, nor what they have done or left undone, but let one examine what one has oneself done or left undone.
Like a beautiful flower, full of color but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.
Like a beautiful flower, full of color and full of scent, are the fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.
As from a heap of flowers many garlands can be made, so many good deeds should be done by one born a mortal.

content.sanskrit: Na pupphagandho vatamanveiti na candanam tagaramallika va
content.text: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor that of sandalwood, tagara, or jasmine. But the fragrance of the virtuous travels even against the wind. The virtuous man pervades all directions.

content.sanskrit: Candanam tagaram vav pi uppalam vassiki capi
content.text: Sandalwood, tagara, lotus, and jasmine—of all these kinds of fragrance, the perfume of virtue is by far the best.
Of little account is the fragrance of tagara and sandalwood, but the fragrance of the virtuous rises up to the gods as the supreme scent.
Mara cannot find the path of those who are possessed of virtue, who live heedfully, and who are liberated by right knowledge.
As upon a heap of rubbish thrown on the highway, a sweet-smelling and lovely lotus may grow,
Even so, among those who are like rubbish, the disciple of the Truly Enlightened One shines forth with wisdom, outshining the blind worldlings.

content.sanskrit: Digha jagarato ratti digham santassa yojanam
content.text: Long is the night to the wakeful; long is the league to the weary. Long is samsara to fools who do not know the sublime Law.
If a traveler does not find an equal or a better companion, let him firmly pursue his journey alone; there is no companionship with a fool.

content.sanskrit: Putta matthi dhanammatthi iti balo vihannati
content.text: 'These sons are mine, this wealth is mine'—thus the fool is afflicted. He himself does not belong to himself; how then sons, how then wealth?

content.sanskrit: Yo balo manyati balyam pandito vapi tena so
content.text: The fool who knows his foolishness is wise to that extent. But the fool who thinks himself wise is indeed called a fool.
Though a fool associates with a wise man all his life, he understands the Dhamma as little as a spoon tastes the soup.
Though a wise man associates with a wise person for only a moment, he quickly understands the Dhamma, as the tongue tastes the soup.
Fools of poor understanding behave to themselves as enemies, doing evil deeds which bear bitter fruit.

content.sanskrit: Na tam kammam katam sadhu yam katva anutappati
content.text: That deed is not well done which, when done, causes regret, and the fruit of which one reaps weeping, with a tearful face.

content.sanskrit: Tanca kammam katam sadhu yam katva nanutappati
content.text: That deed is well done which, when done, causes no regret, and the fruit of which one reaps with joy and delight.

content.sanskrit: Madhuva manyati balo yava papam na paccati
content.text: As long as the evil deed does not ripen, the fool thinks it is like honey. But when the evil deed ripens, the fool suffers grief.

content.sanskrit: Mase mase kusaggena balo bhunjetha bhojanam
content.text: Month after month the fool may eat his food with the tip of a blade of kusa grass; yet he is not worth a sixteenth part of those who have valued the Dhamma.

content.sanskrit: Na hi papam katam kammam saju khiramva muccati
content.text: An evil deed, like freshly drawn milk, does not sour at once. Smoldering like fire covered with ashes, it follows the fool.
When the fool acquires knowledge, it only leads to his ruin. It destroys his good fortune and cleaves his head.
The fool desires false reputation, precedence among monks, authority in monasteries, and honor among other families.
'Let both laymen and monks think that this was done by me; let them refer to me in all decisions'—such is the desire of the fool, and his pride and ambition increase.
One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nibbana. Clearly understanding this, let the monk, the disciple of Buddha, not delight in worldly honor, but strive for solitude.
If one sees a wise man who points out faults and administers reproof as if revealing a treasure, let one follow such a wise man. It is better, not worse, for him who follows such a person.
Let him advise, let him instruct, let him restrain from what is impure. He will be beloved by the good, and hated only by the bad.

content.sanskrit: Na bhaje papake mitte na bhaje purisadhame
content.text: Do not associate with bad friends, do not associate with low men. Associate with good friends, associate with noble men.
He who drinks in the Dhamma lives happily with a serene mind. The wise man always rejoices in the Dhamma, as taught by the Noble Ones.

content.sanskrit: Udakan hi nayanti nettika usukara namayanti tejanam
content.text: Irrigators lead the water; fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters shape the wood; wise people discipline themselves.

content.sanskrit: Sela yatha ekaghano vatena na samirati
content.text: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, so wise people are not moved by praise or blame.
Like a deep lake, clear and calm, so do the wise become serene after hearing the Dhamma.

content.sanskrit: Sabbattha ve sappurisa cajanti na kamakama lapayanti santo
content.text: The good indeed give up attachment for everything. The righteous do not speak with desire for sensual pleasures. Whether touched by happiness or sorrow, the wise show neither elation nor depression.

content.sanskrit: Na attahetu na parahetu na puttamiche na dhanam na rastam
content.text: Neither for one's own sake nor for the sake of another should one desire a son, wealth, or power. One should not desire one's own success by unjust means. Such a person is indeed virtuous, wise, and righteous.

content.sanskrit: Appaka te manussesu ye jana paragamino
content.text: Few are those among men who cross over to the other shore. The rest of mankind only run up and down this side of the bank.

content.sanskrit: Ye ca kho sammadakkhate dhamme dhammanuvattino
content.text: But those who live according to the Dhamma, when the Dhamma has been well-taught, will cross over the dominion of Death, which is difficult to cross.

content.sanskrit: Kanham dhammam vippahaya sukkam bhavetha pandito
content.text: Leaving the way of darkness, let the wise man follow the way of light. Leaving his home for the homeless life, let him seek happiness in solitude, which is difficult to enjoy.
There let him seek delight, having discarded all sensual pleasures, possessing nothing. Let the wise man cleanse himself from all defilements of the mind.

Those whose minds are well-trained in the factors of enlightenment, who without clinging delight in the freedom of attachment, who have destroyed all defilements—they are fully enlightened, and have attained Nibbana in this world.
There is no suffering for him who has completed his journey, who is freed from sorrow, who has freed himself on all sides, who has thrown off all fetters.

content.sanskrit: Uyyunjanti satimanto na nikete ramanti te
content.text: The mindful depart; they do not delight in any home. Like swans that leave their lake, they leave their house and home.
Those who do not accumulate, who reflect well on their food, whose pasture is empty and unconditioned freedom (Nibbana)—their path is difficult to track, like that of birds in the air.
He whose appetites are stilled, who is moderate in food, whose pasture is empty and unconditioned freedom—his path is difficult to track, like that of birds in the air.

content.sanskrit: Yassindriyani samathang gatani assa yatha sarathina sudanta
content.text: Even the gods envy him whose senses are quieted like horses well-broken by a charioteer, who is free from pride, and free from defilements.

content.sanskrit: Pathavisamo no virujjhati indakhilupamo tadi subrato
content.text: Like the earth, he is not troubled by anger; he is firm like a pillar; he is like a lake free from mud. For such a righteous one, there are no further births.

content.sanskrit: Santang tassa manang hoti santa vaca ca kamma ca
content.text: Calm is his mind, calm is his speech, calm is his action, when he has obtained freedom by right knowledge.

content.sanskrit: Assaddho akatannu ca sandhicchedo ca yo naro
content.text: The man who is free from blind faith, who knows the Uncreated, who has cut all bonds, who has ended all temptations—he indeed is the greatest of men.

content.sanskrit: Game va yadi varanye ninne va yadi va thale
content.text: Whether in a village or in a forest, whether in a deep valley or on the mountain, wherever the Arahats dwell, that place is delightful.

content.sanskrit: Ramaneyyani aranyani yattha na ramati jano
content.text: Delightful are the forests where the worldly find no pleasure. There the passionless will find delight, for they seek no sensual pleasures.
Though one should speak a thousand words that are useless, one useful word is better, hearing which one attains peace.
Though one should recite a thousand verses that are useless, one useful stanza is better, hearing which one attains peace.
Though one should recite a hundred verses, if they are useless, one verse of the Dhamma is better, hearing which one attains peace.

content.sanskrit: Yo sahassang sahassena sangame manuse jine
content.text: Though one should conquer in battle a thousand times a thousand men, yet he who conquers himself is indeed the greatest conqueror.

content.sanskrit: Atta have jitam seyyo ya cayam itara paja
content.text: Self-conquest is indeed far better than the conquest of others. Neither a god, nor a gandhabba, nor Mara can defeat the victory of a man who has conquered himself.

content.sanskrit: Yo ca vassasatang jhe sahassena samam yaje
content.text: If a man month after month for a hundred years should offer a thousand sacrifices, and if he but for one moment should pay homage to a self-cultivated man—that homage is far better than a hundred years of sacrifices.

content.sanskrit: Yo ca vassasatang jantu aggim paricare vane
content.text: If a man for a hundred years should worship the sacrificial fire in the forest, and if he but for one moment should pay homage to a self-cultivated man—that homage is far better than a hundred years of worship.

content.sanskrit: Yankinci yitthanva hutanca loke samvatsarang pujaye punnapekkho
content.text: Whatever a man offers in this world as sacrifice or oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, all of it is not worth a quarter. Homage paid to the righteous is far better.
He who always respects and honors the elderly and the virtuous obtains four blessings: long life, beauty, happiness, and strength.

content.sanskrit: Yo ca vassasatang jive dusilo asamahito
content.text: Better than a hundred years lived viciously and uncontrolled is a single day lived virtuously and meditative.
